## Toucanet Of Famous Election

### Game Description

Toucanet Of Famous Election is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board of size 5x5. Players take turns placing their pieces on the board. The goal is to create a line of five pieces in any direction (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally).

### Simplified Rules

The simplified rules of the game are as follows:

* The game is played on a 5x5 square board.
* Players take turns placing their pieces on empty squares.
* The game ends when a player creates a line of five pieces in any direction.

### Ludax Implementation

Here is a simplified implementation of the game in Ludax:
```lark
(game "Toucanet Of Famous Election"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```
This implementation defines a game with two players, a 5x5 square board, and a play phase where players take turns placing their pieces on empty squares. The game ends when a player creates a line of five pieces in any direction or when the board is full.

### Adding More Rules

Let's add more rules to the game. We'll introduce a new rule that allows players to capture an opponent's piece by surrounding it with their own pieces.

```lark
(game "Toucanet Of Famous Election"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (custodial 1) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```
In this updated implementation, players can capture an opponent's piece by surrounding it with their own pieces. The `custodial 1` mask checks for adjacent pieces that can be captured.

### Further Updates

Let's add more features to the game. We'll introduce a scoring system and update the end rules to reflect the scores.

```lark
(game "Toucanet Of Famous Election"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (custodial 1) increment_score:true)
                        (increment_score mover 1)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (by_score))
        )
    )
)
```
In this updated implementation, players earn points for capturing opponent's pieces and for creating lines of five pieces. The game ends when a player reaches a score of 10 or when the board is full, with the player having the highest score winning.

This is a basic implementation of the game, and there are many ways to extend and modify the rules to make the game more interesting and challenging.

## Hilarious Agama Of Opposition

### Game Description

Hilarious Agama Of Opposition is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board of size 5x5. Players take turns placing their pieces on the board. The goal is to create a line of five pieces in any direction (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally).

### Simplified Rules

The simplified rules of the game are as follows:

* The game is played on a 5x5 square board.
* Players take turns placing their pieces on empty squares.
* The game ends when a player creates a line of five pieces in any direction.

### Ludax Implementation

```lark
(game "Hilarious Agama Of Opposition"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Incremental Addition of Rules

#### Rule 1: Capturing Pieces

* When a player places a piece, they can capture an opponent's piece if it is adjacent to the newly placed piece.

```lark
(game "Hilarious Agama Of Opposition"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

#### Rule 2: Scoring

* Players earn points for capturing opponent's pieces.

```lark
(game "Hilarious Agama Of Opposition"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)
                        (increment_score mover 1)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (by_score))    
        )
    )
)
```

#### Rule 3: Forced Pass

* If a player has no valid moves, they must pass their turn.

```lark
(game "Hilarious Agama Of Opposition"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)
                        (increment_score mover 1)
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (by_score))    
        )
    )
)
```

## Tangible Chachalaca Of Experience

### Game Description

Tangible Chachalaca Of Experience is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board of size 5x5. Players take turns placing their pieces on the board. The goal is to create a connected line of pieces that spans from one edge of the board to the opposite edge.

### Game Implementation in Ludax

```ludax
(game "Tangible Chachalaca Of Experience"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge top) (edge bottom))) 1) (mover_is P1)) (mover win))
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge left) (edge right))) 1) (mover_is P2)) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Explanation

* The game is played on a 5x5 square board.
* Players take turns placing their pieces on empty spaces on the board.
* The game ends when a player creates a connected line of pieces that spans from one edge of the board to the opposite edge, or when the board is full.

### Adding Additional Rules

To make the game more interesting, we can add additional rules. For example, we can introduce a "capture" effect that allows a player to capture an opponent's piece by surrounding it with their own pieces.

```ludax
(game "Tangible Chachalaca Of Experience"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (custodial 1) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge top) (edge bottom))) 1) (mover_is P1)) (mover win))
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge left) (edge right))) 1) (mover_is P2)) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Adding Rendering Details

We can also add rendering details to the game, such as assigning colors to the players.

```ludax
(game "Tangible Chachalaca Of Experience"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (custodial 1) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge top) (edge bottom))) 1) (mover_is P1)) (mover win))
            (if (and (>= (connected ((edge left) (edge right))) 1) (mover_is P2)) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

## Delectable Unbiased Mink Game Implementation

The Delectable Unbiased Mink game is a novel two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board with a size of 11x11. The objective of the game is to create a line of five or more pieces in any direction (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally).

### Game Rules

1. The game starts with an empty board.
2. Player 1 (P1) makes the first move, placing a piece on an empty square.
3. Player 2 (P2) makes the second move, placing a piece on an empty square.
4. Players alternate turns.
5. A player can only place a piece on an empty square.
6. The game ends when a player creates a line of five or more pieces in any direction or when the board is full.

### Ludax Implementation

```lark
(game "Delectable Unbiased Mink"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 11))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Explanation

* The game is defined with two players and an 11x11 square board.
* The `play` section defines the game logic, where players take turns placing pieces on empty squares.
* The `end` section defines the game-ending conditions: a player wins if they create a line of five or more pieces, and the game is a draw if the board is full.

### Incremental Additions

To make the game more interesting, we can add additional rules:

* **Capturing pieces**: Introduce a capturing mechanism where a player can capture an opponent's piece by surrounding it with their own pieces.
* **Special pieces**: Introduce special pieces with unique abilities, such as a "blocker" piece that cannot be captured.

These additions can be implemented by modifying the `play` and `end` sections of the game rules.

### Example with Capturing Pieces

```lark
(game "Delectable Unbiased Mink with Capturing"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 11))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (custodial 1) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

In this example, players can capture opponent pieces by surrounding them with their own pieces. The `capture` effect is used to define the capturing mechanism.

Note that this is a basic implementation, and you can modify and extend the game rules to create a more complex and engaging game.

## Solid Diamond Gharial Game Implementation

The Solid Diamond Gharial game is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board with a diamond-shaped grid. The objective of the game is to create a solid diamond shape with your pieces.

### Game Rules

* The game is played on a 9x9 square board with a diamond-shaped grid.
* Each player has 10 pieces.
* Players take turns placing one piece on an empty space on the board.
* A player can only place a piece on a space that is adjacent to one of their existing pieces.
* A player wins if they create a solid diamond shape with their pieces.
* If all spaces on the board are filled and no player has won, the game is a draw.

### Ludax Implementation

```lark
(game "Solid Diamond Gharial"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 9))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (start
            (place P1 (center))
            (place P2 (adjacent (center) direction:up))
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (result 
                        (adjacent (occupied mover))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (and (line 3 exact:true) (connected (corners) mover)) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

### Explanation

* The game is played on a 9x9 square board.
* Each player starts with one piece on the center space and one piece on the space above the center.
* Players take turns placing one piece on an empty space that is adjacent to one of their existing pieces.
* A player wins if they create a solid diamond shape with their pieces, which is checked by verifying that there is a line of 3 pieces and that the pieces are connected to each other.
* If all spaces on the board are filled and no player has won, the game is a draw.

### Incremental Additions

To make the game more complex, we can add additional rules, such as:

* Allowing players to capture opponent pieces by surrounding them.
* Introducing special pieces with unique abilities.
* Adding a scoring system to reward players for creating certain shapes.

These additions can be implemented by modifying the `play` and `end` rules, as well as adding new `effects` and `predicates`. 

For example, to add a capturing mechanism, we can modify the `play` rule to include a `capture` effect:

```lark
(play
    (repeat (P1 P2)
        (place 
            (destination empty)
            (result 
                (adjacent (occupied mover))
            )
            (effects 
                (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
            )
        )
    )
)
```

This will allow players to capture opponent pieces by surrounding them, and increment their score for each captured piece. 

Similarly, to introduce special pieces with unique abilities, we can add new `mover` references and modify the `play` rule to include special effects for those pieces:

```lark
(play
    (repeat (P1 P2)
        (place 
            (destination empty)
            (result 
                (adjacent (occupied mover))
            )
            (effects 
                (if (mover_is P1) (increment_score 2))
                (if (mover_is P2) (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true))
            )
        )
    )
)
```

This will give player 1 a special ability to increment their score by 2 for each piece they place, and give player 2 a special ability to capture opponent pieces. 

These are just a few examples of how the game can be modified and extended. The Ludax language provides a flexible and expressive way to define complex game rules and mechanics.

## Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade

### Game Description

Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board of size 5x5. Players take turns placing their pieces on the board. The goal is to create a line of five pieces in any direction (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally).

### Simplified Rules

The simplified rules of the game are as follows:

* The game is played on a 5x5 square board.
* Players take turns placing their pieces on empty squares.
* The game ends when a player creates a line of five pieces in any direction.

### Ludax Implementation

Here is the Ludax implementation of the simplified rules:

```ludax
(game "Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

### Adding More Rules

Let's add more rules to make the game more interesting. We'll introduce a new piece type called "Fennec" which can move one square in any direction. We'll also add a rule that allows players to upgrade their pieces to Fennec pieces.

```ludax
(game "Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (1 1))
            (place P2 (4 4))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
                (effects
                    (increment_score mover 1)
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (adjacent occupied))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied))
                        (set_score mover (add (score mover) 2))
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

### Upgrading Pieces

To implement the upgrading of pieces, we need to introduce a new piece type called "Fennec" and add a rule that allows players to upgrade their pieces.

```ludax
(game "Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (1 1))
            (place P2 (4 4))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
                (effects
                    (increment_score mover 1)
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (adjacent occupied))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied))
                        (set_score mover (add (score mover) 2))
                    )
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (if (>= (score mover) 5)
                    (effects
                        (upgrade mover Fennec)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

Note that the `upgrade` effect is not a built-in effect in Ludax, so we need to implement it manually. We can do this by introducing a new piece type called "Fennec" and adding a rule that allows players to upgrade their pieces to Fennec pieces.

### Fennec Movement

To implement the Fennec movement, we need to add a new rule that allows Fennec pieces to move one square in any direction.

```ludax
(game "Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade"
    (players 2)
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (1 1))
            (place P2 (4 4))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
                (effects
                    (increment_score mover 1)
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (adjacent occupied))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied))
                        (set_score mover (add (score mover) 2))
                    )
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (if (>= (score mover) 5)
                    (effects
                        (upgrade mover Fennec)
                    )
                )
            )

            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (if (is Fennec mover)
                    (place
                        (destination (adjacent empty))
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

Note that the `is` predicate is not a built-in predicate in Ludax, so we need to implement it manually. We can do this by introducing a new piece type called "Fennec" and adding a rule that checks if a piece is a Fennec piece.

This implementation provides a basic structure for the game Annoying Fennec Of Upgrade. However, it may need to be modified and extended to fully implement the game's rules and mechanics.

## Chestnut Collie From Ganymede

### Game Description

Chestnut Collie From Ganymede is a two-player abstract strategy game. The game is played on a square board of size 5x5. Players take turns placing stones on empty squares. The game ends when one player has five stones in a row, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally.

### Ludax Implementation

```lark
(game "Chestnut Collie From Ganymede"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Adding Start Rules

Let's add a start rule to place the first stone in the center of the board.

```lark
(game "Chestnut Collie From Ganymede"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (start
            (place P1 (3 3))
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Adding Play Effects

Let's add a play effect to capture an opponent's stone when a player places a stone adjacent to it.

```lark
(game "Chestnut Collie From Ganymede"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (start
            (place P1 (3 3))
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

### Adding End Rules

Let's add an end rule to check if a player has five stones in a row.

```lark
(game "Chestnut Collie From Ganymede"
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 5))
    ) 
    
    (rules 
        (start
            (place P1 (3 3))
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place 
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects 
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        
        (end 
            (if (line 5 exact:true) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))    
        )
    )
)
```

prompt:
Invent simple rules for a novel two player abstract strategy game called Slim Expert Frigatebird. Implement it in the ludax language. You will find attached the ludax's grammar as well as a few examples of games implemented in ludax. Start by implementing a simplified version of your rules, and then incrementally add rules that are harder to express in ludax. At each step, make sure you write a compilable game according to ludax's grammar.

        grammar.lark:
// ---Root---
game: "(" "game" name players equipment rules rendering? ")"

// ---Players---
players: "(" "players" positive_int ")"

// ---Equipment---
equipment: "(" "equipment" board ")"
board: "(" "board" (board_square | board_rectangle | board_hexagon | board_hex_rectangle) ")"
board_square: "(" "square" number ")"
board_rectangle: "(" "rectangle" number number ")"
board_hexagon: "(" "hexagon"  number ")"
board_hex_rectangle: "(" "hex_rectangle" number number ")"

// ---Rules---
rules: "(" "rules" start_rules? play_rules end_rules ")"

// ---Start rules---
start_rules: "(" "start" start_rule+ ")"
start_rule: start_place
start_place: "(" "place" player_reference pattern_arg ")"

// ---Play rules---
play_rules: "(" "play" play_phase+ ")"
play_phase: phase_once_through | phase_repeat
phase_once_through: "(" "once_through" play_mover_order play_mechanic ")"
phase_repeat: "(" "repeat" play_mover_order play_mechanic ")"
play_mover_order: "(" player_reference+ ")"

play_mechanic: play_place force_pass? 
play_place: "(" "place" mover_reference? place_destination_constraint place_result_constraint? play_effects?")"
force_pass: "(" "force_pass" ")"

// ---Constraints---
place_destination_constraint: "(" "destination" super_mask ")"
place_result_constraint: "(" "result" super_predicate ")"

// ---Effects---
play_effects: "(" "effects" play_effect+ ")"
play_effect: effect_capture 
           | effect_flip
           | effect_increment_score
           | effect_set_score

effect_capture: "(" "capture" super_mask mover_reference? increment_score_arg? ")"
effect_flip: "(" "flip" super_mask mover_reference? ")"
effect_increment_score: "(" "increment_score" mover_reference function ")"
effect_set_score: "(" "set_score" mover_reference function ")"

// ---Functions---
function: function_add
        | function_connected
        | function_constant
        | function_count
        | function_line
        | function_multiply
        | function_score
        | function_subtract

function_add: "(" "add" function+ ")"
function_connected: "(" "connected" multi_mask_arg mover_reference? direction_arg? ")"
function_constant: positive_int
function_count: "(" "count" super_mask ")"
function_line: "(" "line" positive_int orientation_arg? exact_arg? ")"
function_multiply: "(" "multiply" function+ ")"
function_score: "(" "score" mover_reference ")"
function_subtract: "(" "subtract" function function ")"

// ---End rules---
end_rules: "(" "end" end_rule+ ")"
end_rule: "(" "if" super_predicate end_rule_result ")"
?end_rule_result: result_win | result_lose | result_draw | result_by_score

// -- Result definitions --
result_win: "(" mover_reference "win" ")"
result_lose: "(" mover_reference "lose" ")"
result_draw: "(" "draw" ")"
result_by_score: "(" "by_score" ")"

// -- Mask definitions --
super_mask: mask | super_mask_and | super_mask_or | super_mask_not
super_mask_and: "(" "and" super_mask+ ")"
super_mask_or: "(" "or" super_mask+ ")"
super_mask_not: "(" "not" super_mask ")"

mask: mask_adjacent
    | mask_center
    | mask_corners
    | mask_custodial
    | mask_edge
    | mask_empty
    | mask_occupied
    | mask_pattern
    | mask_prev_move

mask_adjacent: "(" "adjacent" super_mask direction_arg? ")"
mask_center: "center"
mask_corners: "corners"
mask_custodial: "(" "custodial" custodial_length_arg mover_reference? orientation_arg? ")"
mask_edge: "(" "edge" edge ")"
mask_empty: "empty"
mask_occupied: "occupied" | "(" "occupied" mover_reference ")"
mask_pattern: "(" "pattern" dimensions_arg pattern_arg rotate_arg? ")"
mask_prev_move: "(" "prev_move" mover_reference ")"

// "Multi-masks" are special keywords that are manually split into multiple
// sub-masks at compile time. This is mostly useful for the "connected" function,
// which expects a list of masks to check for connections between
multi_mask: multi_mask_corners
          | multi_mask_edges
          | multi_mask_edges_no_corners

multi_mask_corners: "corners"
multi_mask_edges: "edges"
multi_mask_edges_no_corners: "edgesNoCorners"

// ---Predicate definitions---
super_predicate: predicate | super_predicate_and | super_predicate_or | super_predicate_not
super_predicate_and: "(" "and" super_predicate+ ")"
super_predicate_or: "(" "or" super_predicate+ ")"
super_predicate_not: "(" "not" super_predicate ")"

predicate: predicate_equals
         | predicate_exists
         | predicate_full_board
         | predicate_function
         | predicate_greater_equals
         | predicate_less_equals
         | predicate_mover_is
         | predicate_passed

predicate_equals: "(" "=" function+ ")"
predicate_exists: "(" "exists" super_mask ")" // technically equivalent to (>= (count mask) 1) 
predicate_full_board: "(" "full_board" ")"
predicate_function: function // special syntax which is equivalent to "(>= function 1)"
predicate_greater_equals: "(" ">=" function function ")"
predicate_less_equals: "(" "<=" function function ")"
predicate_mover_is: "(" "mover_is" player_reference ")"
predicate_passed: "(" "passed" (mover_reference | BOTH) ")"

// Additional (potentially optional) arguments for predicates
custodial_length_arg: ANY | positive_int
dimensions_arg: "(" positive_int positive_int ")"
direction_arg: "direction:" direction
exact_arg: "exact:" boolean
increment_score_arg: "increment_score:" boolean
multi_mask_arg: multi_mask | "(" super_mask+ ")"
orientation_arg: "orientation:" orientation
pattern_arg: "(" positive_int+ ")"
rotate_arg: "rotate:" boolean

// Optional rendering details
rendering: "(" "rendering" rendering_detail+ ")"
rendering_detail: color_assignment

color_assignment: "(" "color" player_reference color ")"

// General-purpose definitions
?number: SIGNED_NUMBER
?positive_int: /[0-9]+/
?boolean: TRUE | FALSE
?edge: TOP | BOTTOM | LEFT | RIGHT | TOP_LEFT | TOP_RIGHT | BOTTOM_LEFT | BOTTOM_RIGHT
?direction: UP | DOWN | LEFT | RIGHT | UP_LEFT | UP_RIGHT | DOWN_LEFT | DOWN_RIGHT | VERTICAL | HORIZONTAL | ORTHOGONAL | DIAGONAL | BACK_DIAGONAL | FORWARD_DIAGONAL | ANY
?orientation: VERTICAL | HORIZONTAL | ORTHOGONAL | DIAGONAL | BACK_DIAGONAL | FORWARD_DIAGONAL | ANY
?color: WHITE | BLACK
// ----------------------------

?player_reference: P1 | P2
?mover_reference: MOVER | OPPONENT
name: STRING
variable_name: /\?[a-z][a-z0-9]*/
id: /[a-zA-Z0-9_]+/

// Constants
TOP: "top"
BOTTOM: "bottom"
UP: "up"
DOWN: "down"
LEFT: "left"
RIGHT: "right"
TOP_LEFT: "top_left"
TOP_RIGHT: "top_right"
BOTTOM_LEFT: "bottom_left"
BOTTOM_RIGHT: "bottom_right"
UP_LEFT: "up_left"
UP_RIGHT: "up_right"
DOWN_LEFT: "down_left"
DOWN_RIGHT: "down_right"
VERTICAL: "vertical"
HORIZONTAL: "horizontal"
ORTHOGONAL: "orthogonal"
DIAGONAL: "diagonal"
BACK_DIAGONAL: "back_diagonal"
FORWARD_DIAGONAL: "forward_diagonal"
ANY: "any"
TRUE: "true"
FALSE: "false"
MOVER: "mover"
OPPONENT: "opponent"
P1: "P1"
P2: "P2"
BOTH: "both"
WHITE: "white"
BLACK: "black"

// Lark imports
%import common.ESCAPED_STRING -> STRING
%import common.SIGNED_NUMBER
%import common.WS
%ignore WS


        example games:
        

(game "Complexity Demo" 
    (players 2)
    (equipment 
        (board (square 10))

        (play
            (once_through (P1)
                (place (destination empty))

        (end

            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))

    )
)

)
